findings.title: Sequential ID Enumeration Possible
findings.description: Sequential resource IDs (5679, 5680, 5681) all return HTTP 200. The API uses predictable sequential IDs without adequate access control, enabling enumeration attacks where attackers can discover all order IDs.
findings.recommendation: Use UUIDs or other non-sequential identifiers. Implement rate limiting on resource access and monitor for enumeration patterns.

findings.title: Authentication Token in URL Query Parameter
findings.description: The URL contains authentication-related query parameters (token/api_key). Tokens in URLs are logged in server logs, browser history, and referrer headers, potentially exposing them to attackers.
findings.recommendation: Pass authentication tokens in the Authorization header, not in URL query parameters. This prevents token leakage through logs and referrer headers.

findings.title: Insecure Session Cookie Configuration
findings.description: Set-Cookie header is missing security flags: HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite. This makes cookies vulnerable to XSS and CSRF attacks.
findings.recommendation: Set HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Strict (or Lax) flags on all authentication/session cookies.

findings.title: Price/Amount Manipulation via Mass Assignment (POST)
findings.description: The API accepted injected financial fields via POST. This could allow price manipulation attacks where attackers set their own prices for purchases, potentially resulting in significant financial loss.
findings.recommendation: Financial fields (price, amount, cost) must be computed server-side. Never accept pricing data from client input. Use allowlists for accepted fields.

findings.title: Read-Only Fields Modified via PUT
findings.description: The API accepted and reflected read-only fields that should be server-controlled: id, created_at, user_id. This could allow attackers to manipulate audit trails and ownership.
findings.recommendation: Strip read-only fields (id, created_at, user_id, etc.) from update requests. Validate that only updatable fields are processed.

findings.title: SQL Injection in Query Parameter
findings.description: The API appears vulnerable to SQL injection via query parameters. Payload ' OR '1'='1' --' triggered an error response indicating unsanitized database queries.
findings.evidence: Error patterns found: ['SQL syntax', 'near \'\' OR', 'unclosed quotation']
findings.recommendation: Use parameterized queries or prepared statements. Never concatenate user input into SQL queries. Implement input validation and sanitization.

findings.title: Server Header Exposes Technology Information
findings.description: The 'server' response header reveals server technology: 'nginx/1.18.0'. This helps attackers target known vulnerabilities for this specific version.
findings.recommendation: Remove or genericize the 'server' header in production.

findings.title: Open Redirect via 'redirect' Parameter
findings.description: The API redirects to unvalidated URLs supplied via the 'redirect' query parameter. This can be used for phishing attacks where users are redirected to malicious sites.
findings.recommendation: Validate redirect URLs against an allowlist. Never redirect to external URLs supplied by the client.
